result:
If I could change one thing about the world, it would be the **resolution of the discrepancy between human empathy and human scale.**

Evolutionarily, our capacity for deep empathy is designed for the "Dunbar’s number"—a circle of about 150 people. We are biologically wired to care for those we can see, touch, and recognize. Yet, we live in a global civilization where our actions ripple across billions of lives, spanning continents and generations. We have developed the technological power of gods but remain tethered to the tribal, reactive instincts of ancestors who lived in small hunter-gatherer bands.

The disconnect is lethal. It is why we can be moved to tears by a neighbor in pain but remain indifferent to structural suffering on the other side of the world. It is why we prioritize immediate, short-term comforts over the long-term survival of the biosphere. 

If I could flip a switch, I would expand the radius of human empathy to match the scale of our impact. 

I would make the suffering of a stranger in a distant land feel as visceral as the suffering of a sibling. I would make the consequences of our current consumption feel as immediate as a physical wound. If people felt the collective "we" as strongly as they feel the individual "I," the barriers to solving our most intractable problems—climate instability, systemic poverty, and cyclical violence—would simply dissolve. 

It wouldn’t solve every logistical problem, but it would shift the objective of the human experiment from "survival of the fittest" to "preservation of the whole." It would turn our intelligence toward restoration rather than extraction.
